system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 15: Section Three/Part 7: Fun Jazz

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 15 — Section Three/Part 7: Fun Jazz

_Pages 168-184._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Imply more with less

> In all of the tunes here | like to imply more with less. Chords, for instance, are most often only
> two or three notes, and the melody is all-important.

**Significance (p. 168).** This is the author's core aesthetic rule for fingerstyle jazz arrangement, stating that sparse voicings and melodic primacy are the guiding principle throughout these pieces.

## Descending chromatic key change

> The key changes from C to D moving chromatically by half step. Don’t let the chord quality get in
> the way of your analysis. It can be simple or sophisticated. It also does not have to have its root in the
> bottom position as it does here.

**Significance (p. 167).** This passage defines a specific modulation technique and gives a rule about analytical flexibility — chord quality and bass position are secondary to recognizing the chromatic movement.

## Approaching by half step

> Approaching a chord from a half step above or belowis another accepted way of moving the harmony.

**Significance (p. 167).** This defines a standard harmonic approach technique (chromatic neighbor chords) as a recognized device for voice-leading between keys.

## Within-scale cycle / deceptive cadence

> A major scale can within itself provide cyclical movement, although not with
> exact perfect fifth intervals throughout. This within-the-scale cycle is used here
> through the Dm7, after which a’ VI (A’6) of the C scale is inserted. This deceptive
> cadence opens up several possibilities

**Significance (p. 167).** This explains the concept of diatonic cycle-of-fifths movement and the use of a deceptive cadence (bVI substitution) as a modulation tool.

## Community standards / ear rule

> Ultimately, whatever sounds good to the ear will probably work. Just go there. Of course, if all
> your playing buddies grimace, you do have to remember that there are community standards of
> decency.

**Significance (p. 167).** This gives the author's overarching rule for harmonic decision-making: ear first, but tempered by shared musical convention.

## Good Evening Friends / triplet feel

> There are three independent voices at once in this piece, if you count a whole note as a voice. It is. Play the piece with an underlying triplet feel.

**Significance (p. 170).** This is a direct performance instruction establishing both the three-voice texture and the rhythmic feel required to execute the piece correctly.

## Stride to bebop transition

> This Fats Waller imitation starts with a solid stride style. After a while I sneak bebop into it. Ragtime bebop? Why not?

**Significance (p. 171).** This labels the stylistic technique of layering bebop vocabulary over a stride foundation, defining the compositional approach of the piece for the player.

## Ballad / double time feel

> I love ballads. They allow a player to have as much elbow room to do whatever he wants, such as playing in double time feel.

**Significance (p. 174).** This introduces double-time feel as a specific technique available within a ballad context, giving the player a license and method for rhythmic variation.

## Sparse bass / eighth note flow

> This blues is sparse in bass notes yet feels as though nothing is missing. One way this is accom-
> plished is by using an almost continual eighth note flow. Joe Pass often used this device.

**Significance (p. 177).** This defines a specific compensatory technique — sustained eighth-note melodic flow — for maintaining rhythmic momentum when bass notes are sparse, and attributes it to Joe Pass.

## Single-line to fingerstyle arrangement

> That was a book for pick guitar, and the melodic line was just one note with
> no accompaniment. Here | fleshed it out and turned it into a self-accompanied fingerstyle
> arrangement. You can do this with virtually any existing tune you have been playing as a single
> line only.

**Significance (p. 177).** This gives a transferable method — converting any single-line melody into a self-accompanied fingerstyle arrangement — making it a generalizable technique, not just a description of one piece.

## 3 on 4 / tritone accompaniment

> 3 on 4 takes its name from having many three-beat phrases in the context of 4/4 measures. The melodic line is primarily accompanied by tri-tones. This is the type of sparse playing that could easily fit in a trio or quartet setting.

**Significance (p. 179).** This defines both the rhythmic concept (polymetric three-beat phrasing over 4/4) and the harmonic technique (tritone accompaniment) that characterize this piece and its applicable performance context.

## Walkdown defined

> A walkdown is a descending bass line with a common tone on top of a chord stack. This is the
>
> device that keeps popping up in this tune along with its hook-like rhythm.

**Significance (p. 181).** This provides a precise definition of the walkdown technique — a core fingerstyle jazz device — and identifies it as the structural hook of the piece.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
